Current research indicates that AIs can already perform around average SAT math performance (e.g., the GeoS system achieved an estimated SAT math score equivalent to 500 out of 800 in 2015). Achieving the 75th percentile score (~650+) requires handling diverse topics like algebra, coordinate geometry, statistics, and multi-step problems — more complex than the narrow focus of the geometry problems solved by past systems like GeoS. With seven years (from 2017 to 2025) for AI progress and increased focus in areas like natural language understanding and mathematical reasoning (especially from organizations like AI2), it is increasingly plausible such performance will be surpassed. Additionally, the cited 2025 NY Times article shows that AI-related forecasts and discussions by 2025 emphasize rapidly advancing AIs, supporting increased capacity.
temporal_anchors: 2015 - Allen AI2 GeoS SAT Geometry System; 2022 - Japan NII's Tokyo U project failed; 2025 - Relevant timeline in NYT opinion article
